events.content:
Marylebone High Street. The shops here have clean windows and painted signs. A butcher, a chemist, a bookseller with a bow window full of stacked volumes. The pavement is wide enough for two people to pass without stepping into the road.

Ida moves through it carefully. She is conspicuous here in a different way than at King's Cross — not invisible, but wrong. The wrong clothes, the wrong pace. She keeps moving.

A narrow shop, floor-to-ceiling shelves on three walls, a glass counter at the back behind which Marchetti sits with a catalogue open in front of him. The smell of old paper and binding glue. The bell above the door rings when anyone enters.

Marchetti looks up when Ida comes in. He has the practised neutrality of a man who sees everyone who enters this shop and registers them without appearing to.

A coffee house on a side street off Baker Street. The kind of place where professional men come to read papers and conduct business that does not quite merit an office. Dark wood, low ceiling, the smell of roasting beans and pipe tobacco.

On Tuesdays, between nine and half past, a solicitor's clerk sits at the third table from the window. He always leaves his bag on the floor beside the chair. He always orders the same thing and reads for twenty-five minutes. He is entirely predictable, which is his professional vulnerability.

tags: The office is occupied. Wednesday is the only clear day.
events.content:
A first-floor office above a tobacconist on Baker Street. The stairs creak but the tobacconist below is hard of hearing and does not look up from his counter.

The office is a working solicitor's room — files, ledgers, correspondence in stacked trays. The smell of ink and dust and the faint sweetness from the tobacco shop below. On Wednesdays, between ten and one, the solicitor is at Gray's Inn. The clerks eat lunch at noon.

One shelf, second from the left, holds ledgers in green binding.

The residential streets behind Baker Street. Terraced houses, small private gardens behind iron railings, the occasional mews. Quieter than the High Street, the kind of quiet that comes from money having nothing to prove.

Fenn lives somewhere on these streets. Ida has not been told which house. That is deliberate.

tags: Something has been left here for Ida.
events.content:
The third shelf from the bottom on the east wall, half-hidden behind a row of outdated directories. Marchetti never touches this shelf. The dust on it is undisturbed except for a gap, small and deliberate, where things are occasionally placed and collected.

*Leave* an item here to deposit it. *Examine* to collect what is waiting.

tags: The clasp opens. Inside, among papers and a lunch wrapped in cloth, a folded document with a blue cover and a red seal.
The bag is on the floor beside the chair. There is no reason to touch it today.
events.content:
A brown leather bag, good quality but worn, sitting on the floor beside the clerk's chair. He has taken his newspaper to the counter to ask for more coffee. He will be back in four minutes.

The clasp is not locked. It could be *searched*.

tags: The ledger falls open at a page of names and amounts. One name appears three times under different headings. She traces it with her finger.
events.content:
Six green ledgers, uniform binding, each labelled on the spine in a small neat hand. The dates run back twelve years. The most recent is on the right.

She was told to find a specific name. She was given a key — from the clockmaker's forged papers — that tells her which column to look in. *Read* the ledger.

A man of about thirty, well-dressed without ostentation — the clothes of someone who wants to be taken seriously rather than noticed. He moves with the particular economy of a person who has learned to waste nothing, including expression.

He is at Baker Street platform when Ida first arrives. He has been there for some time. He was watching the platform. Then he was watching her.

A man of indeterminate age behind the counter, small, with the patience of someone who spends most of his time waiting for people who may not come. He is always doing something — cataloguing, writing, examining a spine — but gives the impression that the real task is simply being present in the shop.

He does not ask questions. This is his most important quality.

tags: He takes his newspaper to the counter to order more coffee.
events.content:
A man in his forties, neatly dressed, with ink on his right index finger that he has not entirely scrubbed away. He reads with the focused attention of someone who has learned to use small windows of time efficiently.

On Tuesdays he is here from nine to half past. He always leaves his bag on the floor. He always orders a second coffee at twenty past and takes his newspaper to the counter while it is prepared.

That is four minutes.

A man stationed near the top of Baker Street station steps, calling rates at anyone who emerges. He has been doing this since the station opened and has developed an efficient patter that names a price before the potential fare has finished blinking in the daylight.

Ida is beneath his notice. He looks through her.

tags: How long have you been watching?
events.content:
He falls into step beside her on the platform, unhurried, as though they were already mid-conversation.

*You've been doing Callow's work for three months*, he says. Not accusatory — informational. *You're careful, you don't panic, and you read. That last part is unusual.* He glances at her sideways. *I have a different kind of work. It pays better.*

He stops walking and faces her.

*Since King's Cross*, he says. *The day you lifted the wallet.* A pause. *Not for Callow. For myself.* He says it without apology. *I wanted to see if you were what he said you were.*

*You are.*

*My name is Fenn*, he says. *I work for people who deal in paper — documents, records, information. Things that change hands quietly.* He looks at her steadily. *The kind of work I'm describing requires someone who can enter a coffee house on a Tuesday morning and leave with something that was never there.*

He holds out a letter. His name and an address on Baker Street, printed on the front.

*Read it*, he says. *Come to the address if you decide yes. Don't come if you decide no, and we'll say this conversation didn't happen.*

He walks away before she can answer.

Fenn receives Ida with the same manner he uses for everything — correct, measured, neither warm nor cold. He has looked at the document she retrieved and placed it in a leather folder without comment.

*Clean work*, he says. The same words Callow and Rook use. She is beginning to think it is the only professional compliment this city offers.

He tells her. The instructions are more detailed than Callow's — Fenn explains the why as well as the what, to the extent that it helps her perform the job correctly. She realises that Callow withheld the why as a matter of control. Fenn gives it as a matter of efficiency.

Both methods work. They reflect different theories of how people function.

*People who need things to happen quietly*, he says. *The law. Commerce. Occasionally medicine.* He looks at her. *The Quill handles things that cannot be handled officially. You understand the distinction.*

It is not a question. He has already decided she understands it.

*They contain what they contain*, he says. *Your job is to move them, not to understand them.* A pause. *Although I suspect you read them anyway.* He says it without accusation — more like noting a variable he has already accounted for. *That's fine. Information you can't use isn't dangerous.*

He looks at her levelly. *The information you could use — you won't find it yet.*

tags: Tell me about the green ledger.
events.content:
Fenn is quiet for a moment when Ida returns the ledger key. Something shifts in his assessment — not surprise, exactly. More like the confirmation of a calculation he had already made.

*You found the name*, he says. It is not a question.

*Most people miss it*, he says. *It appears in three different columns under three different headings. You have to know what you're looking for.* He pauses. *Or be very thorough.*

*The ledger is a record of payments*, he says. *Certain payments that were made through this office on behalf of clients who required discretion.* He chooses his words carefully. *The name you found appears as both a recipient and an authorising party. That is unusual. It means the same person was paying and being paid — which is only possible if they were operating on both sides of something.*

He stops there. That is all he will say today.

A pause. Brief but present.

*A solicitor with the Metropolitan Railway Company*, he says. *He handles land acquisition. Contracts.* Another pause. *He is very good at his work.*

The way he says it makes it sound like a warning.

Fenn looks at her when she comes in. He looks at her hands. Then back at her face.

*The ledger page*, he says. *Is it gone?*

The question is quiet. The weight behind it is not.

She says nothing.

Fenn is still for a long moment. Then he does something she has not seen him do before — he looks away. At the window, at the street below, at something that is not in this room.

*You understand what that means*, he says. Not anger. Something more precise than anger. *For you. For your family. For the arrangement we have had.*

He turns back. His face is exactly as it always is.

*I hope it's worth it*, he says. *I genuinely do.*

*Good*, he says. He makes a note. The note is brief — a single line, then the folder closed.

*Then we're finished with that matter.* He looks up. *There's other work, if you want it.*

He says it as though nothing has happened. Because as far as he is concerned, nothing has.
He looks up from his catalogue. *Yes?* He waits. He will wait as long as is necessary.

He names a title and a price without consulting anything. He either has it or he doesn't. If he doesn't, he knows where to find it. He has been in this shop for twenty years and he knows the inventory the way Ida knows the platform at Farringdon.

He does not ask why she wants it. He does not ask anything.

He watches her move to the shelf. He goes back to his catalogue.

When she leaves, he does not look up.

He does not look at her. She is not the kind of person he looks at in coffee houses. He turns a page.

The bag is on the floor. The clasp faces outward.

He has taken his newspaper to the counter. His back is to the room. The coffee is being prepared — she can hear the sound of it.

Four minutes. The bag is on the floor beside the empty chair.
He is in the middle of calling a rate at a man in a good coat and does not break off. Ida passes through his field of vision and out the other side without registering.

A folded document, blue cover stock, sealed with red wax — the seal already broken, or perhaps it was never sealed properly, the wax applied for appearance rather than security.

Inside: dense legal text, a list of names, a column of figures. She reads it once, quickly, on the train west. She cannot tell what it means. She can tell it means something.

A single folded sheet. *A. Fenn, Solicitor's Clerk* printed at the top, an address on Baker Street below. The body of the letter is brief — it describes a business arrangement in terms that could mean almost anything. The final line: *The work pays six shillings per task, on completion.*

Six shillings is more than Callow has paid her for anything.

A small folded card from the clockmaker. On one side, what looks like a note about a watch repair. On the other, a grid of columns and headings — a key for reading the solicitor's ledger, which uses a private coding system for sensitive entries.

With this, the ledger becomes readable. Without it, the relevant entries are invisible.

Three times, in three different columns, under three different headings. The same name.

**Harwick.**

Once as a recipient. Once as an authorising party. Once — in the oldest entry — as a witness to a transaction that the ledger does not otherwise describe.

She does not know who Harwick is. She puts the ledger back exactly as she found it and carries the name out of the office.

The Quill does not employ people. It retains them. The distinction matters: employment creates obligation and record. Retention creates deniability.

Fenn does not know Ida's real name. He knows her by a description and a meeting place. If she disappeared tomorrow, the Quill's ledgers would show nothing — no name, no payment, no connection. She is a resource that cannot be audited.

This cuts both ways.

Two full journeys, east and west, listening.

The journalist boarded at Portland Road. He spoke to no one directly. But he read from a notebook — fragments visible when he turned pages — and muttered to himself in the way of a man organising an argument.

She caught three things: a street name in Clerkenwell. The words *compulsory purchase*. And a date — six months from now.

She delivers the account to Fenn exactly as observed. His expression does not change. But he writes for longer than usual.

Fenn approached Ida on Baker Street platform. He has a letter and an address. The work pays six shillings per task.

She has not yet decided whether to go.

A solicitor's clerk at a coffee house off Baker Street, Tuesdays between nine and half past. Blue document, red seal, in the bag he leaves on the floor. Lift it, don't crease it, deliver it to the dead-drop shelf at Marchetti's Books on Marylebone High Street.

Ride the full line twice — east and west — on a specific day. A journalist will board at different stations. Don't approach him. Listen. Remember everything he says or reads aloud. Report back to Fenn with a full account.
She has made both journeys. She has the account. Return to Fenn.

tags: The ledger key is needed to read the coded entries.
events.content: A solicitor's office on Baker Street, first floor, above the tobacconist. Wednesday, between ten and one. Green ledger, second shelf from the left. Find a specific name — the clockmaker's key tells her which column. Memorise the entry. Replace the ledger exactly. Don't take it.

Deliver a letter to a woman at Portland Road station, Wednesday morning train from Paddington. Grey dress, travelling alone. The letter goes into her hand only — no one else's. If anyone is watching the woman, abort and report back.

A clerk is being blackmailed by someone in the Shambles. The money moves through three hands before it reaches its destination — all on the railway. Follow it without the carriers knowing they are watched. Multiple journeys, possibly multiple days. Find the destination.

tags: Side street off the High Street.
The office is occupied. Wednesday is the only clear day.
